citation_sentences: Flat dog-bone-shaped tensile samples were machined from the sheets using wire electrical discharge machining (EDM), with their tensile axes parallel to the rolling direction. A schematic of the shape and dimensions of the tensile test samples is provided in Fig. 1. The sample surfaces were mechanically polished and subsequently electro-polished in a solution of 8 vol.% perchloric acid in ethanol for 30 s at 25 V to remove surface stresses and scratches. Uniaxially tensile tests were performed at room temperature using a universal testing machine (Shimadzu AG-Xplus 250 kN) under a constant strain rate of $1 0 ^ { - 3 } s ^ { - 1 }$ .

Flat dog-bone-shaped fatigue samples were also extracted from the sheets via EDM, with the loading axis parallel to the rolling direction. These samples shared the same dimensions as the tensile samples (Fig. 1). The surfaces were prepared using the same procedure of mechanical grinding and electropolishing to ensure a stress-free and smooth finish. Stress-controlled tension–tension fatigue tests were conducted in ambient air using a servo-hydraulic fatigue testing machine (Shimadzu EHF-EV050K2A-020-1). A sinusoidal waveform was adopted in fatigue tests at a frequency of 20 Hz and a stress ratio of $R = 0 . 1$ .

citation_sentences: Engineering stress–strain curves for the ST and aged samples are shown in Fig. 4, and the corresponding data of tensile properties are listed in Table 1. All aged conditions demonstrate significant increases in both yield and ultimate tensile strengths but a reduction in ductility, compared with the ST condition. Among the aged samples, 550-6 and 600-6 samples demonstrate superior strengths, whereas the 650-6 sample shows a marked reduction in strength. The strengthening mechanism primarily arises from precipitation hardening by intragranular κ-carbides through their interactions with dislocations during deformation. Conversely, the diminished strength in the 650-6 sample is attributed to grain boundary weakening caused by serious intergranular κ-carbide precipitation [6,24].

citation_sentences: According to Eq. (1), the Δσ/2-2N curves of the Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C steel are approximately linear in the finite-life region under doublelogarithmic coordinates, as shown in Fig. 5. The fitted fatigue strength coefficient $\sigma _ { \mathrm { { f } } } ^ { \prime }$ and exponent b, together with the root mean square error of log $N _ { \mathrm { f } } ,$ are summarized in Table 1. At a given stress amplitude, all aged samples exhibit longer fatigue lives compared with the ST sample. The fatigue strength, defined as the maximum stress amplitude sustained for $1 0 ^ { 7 }$ cycles without failure, was measured to be 157.5 MPa for ST, 162 MPa for 550-6, 175.5 MPa for 600-6, and 171 MPa for 650-6. Apparently, the aging treatment effectively enhances the fatigue resistance at low stress amplitudes, with the 600-6 sample delivering the highest fatigue strength. It is further noted that the 550-6 condition exhibits the longest fatigue lives among the aged samples at higher stress amplitudes, whereas at lower stress amplitudes close to the fatigue limit, the 600-6 condition attains the highest fatigue lives and fatigue strength. This apparent difference between low and high stress behavior is rationalized in Sections 4.2 and 4.3 in terms of the competition between intragranular precipitation strengthening and grain boundary weakening.

citation_sentences: At the final fracture stage, the rapid fracture zones of the ST, 550-6, and 600-6 samples are predominantly characterized by dimples (Fig. 8a-$\mathrm { c , e { - } g ) , }$ , indicating a microvoid coalescence mechanism and ductile failure. In contrast, the 650-6 sample displays mixed fracture characteristics (Fig. 8d,h), featuring both dimples and cleavage faceted regions. These facets are typically associated with reduced ductility, which is primarily attributed to the extensive precipitation of intergranular κ-carbides in the 650-6 sample.

paper_title: Fatigue properties of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel: Critical impact of κ-carbide precipitation state
citation_sentences: To elucidate the crack-path characteristics of the fatigued samples, EBSD-based band contrast (BC) and IPF superimposed maps, Kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps, and Schmid factor (SF) maps were acquired adjacent to the main cracks at a stress amplitude of 225 MPa (Fig. 9). The BC and IPF superimposed images $( \mathrm { F i g . ~ } 9 \mathrm { a } , \mathrm { d } , \mathrm { g } , \mathrm { j } )$ reveal that cracks in all four samples propagate along slip bands in a transgranular mode; it is consistent with crack advance along favorably oriented {111} slip planes in face-centered cubic (FCC) alloys [31–34]. Despite this common fracture mode, clear differences in path geometry are evident. In the ST sample, the crack path within large austenite grains is relatively straight. In contrast, the aged samples (550-6, 600-6, and 650-6) exhibit significantly greater path tortuosity and frequent microbranching as the crack interacts with the heterogeneous slip field. It should be noted, however, that the κ-carbides are nanoscale precipitates, whereas the fatigue crack path resolved by EBSD is on the micrometer scale. Given the step size and spatial resolution employed, individual intragranular κ-carbides cannot be resolved in the EBSD maps, making it impossible to establish a one-to-one correspondence between single precipitates and local crack deflections. Instead, the influence of κ-carbide precipitation is reflected at a more global level through its effect on the dominant slip mode, the degree of strain localization, and the resulting crack-path morphology. Based on the increased branching and deflection observed in the aged samples, we infer that the intragranular κ-carbides act as effective nanoscale obstacles that impede dislocation movement and redistribute the local crack-tip stress field. This process promotes the activation of multiple slip systems, thereby inducing crack deflection or branching and enhancing crack propagation resistance [35].

KAM maps (Fig. 9b,e,h,k) provide additional insight into the local plastic strain fields developed around the fatigue cracks. It is well established that the local misorientation measured by EBSD serves as a reliable quantitative indicator of micro-scale plastic strain. In the present alloy, high KAM values are primarily concentrated in the immediate vicinity of the crack path and at grain boundaries, particularly those oriented approximately perpendicular to the loading axis. This distribution reflects impeded slip transmission and the resulting dislocation pile-ups during cyclic loading. The ST sample exhibits the highest KAM intensity and the steepest gradients at these boundaries, indicative of severe strain localization. In contrast, the 550-6 and, most notably, the 600-6 samples display more moderate KAM levels with a more diffuse misorientation distribution around the crack tip. This suggests that the uniformly dispersed intragranular κ-carbides facilitate more homogeneous plastic accommodation, thereby alleviating strain incompatibility at grain boundaries. Conversely, in the 650-6 sample, localized KAM “hot spots” reappear at certain grain boundaries decorated with intergranular κ-carbides (indicated by red arrows). These hot spots point to intense local strain concentration in these regions, which is consistent with the reduced fatigue life of the 650-6 sample compared with the 600-6 and 550-6 conditions.

SF maps (Fig. 9c,f,i,l) indicate a classical sequence for crack initiation and propagation pattern, namely, cracks tend to nucleate in “hard-oriented” grains (low SF) due to limited slip activity and high local stress concentration, and then propagate into adjacent “soft-oriented” grains (high SF) where dislocation slip is more easily activated, providing a low-energy path for crack advancement. Overall, the κ-carbide–induced tortuosity/branching, combined with the reduced grain-boundary strain localization, collectively enhance the crack-propagation resistance in the aged samples. A comparable microstructural scenario has been reported in Ni-based IN718, where nanoscale coherent $\gamma "$ precipitates modify the slip mode and crack-path tortuosity during fatigue crack growth, thereby strongly affecting the fatigue crack propagation

citation_sentences: 315 MPa are shown in Fig. 10. In the ST sample, local wavy slip (Fig. 10a) is observed at $\Delta \sigma / 2 = 1 8 0 ~ \mathrm { M P a }$ . The wavy slip morphology results from screw dislocations undergoing cross slip after pile-up, leading to the formation of step-like slip bands. This irreversible slip intensifies local strain concentration and accelerates crack initiation [37,38]. At the higher stress amplitude of 315 MPa, stress localization in the ST sample becomes more severe and kink bands are formed (Fig. 10b), indicative of a loss of deformability due to highly localized and irreversible deformation.

In contrast, the 600-6 sample exhibits distinct, uniformly spaced slip bands at both stress amplitudes of 180 MPa and 315 MPa (Fig. 10c,d). These slip bands are actually planar slip bands that are attributed to the “glide plane softening” mechanism as discussed in Section 4.1. At Δσ/2 = 315 MPa, the spacing between slip bands decreases and the cooperative activation of multiple slip systems accommodates plastic strain more uniformly. As a result, deformation proceeds via a dense yet largely reversible network of slip bands, effectively preventing catastrophic strain localization such as kink bands [19,38,39]. In summary, the presence of intragranular κ-carbides promotes reversible planar slip and thereby enhances the fatigue deformation response of the 600-6 sample across the studied cyclic stress levels.

paper_abstract: Department of Materials Physics and Chemistry, School of Materials Science and Engineering, and Key Laboratory for Anisotropy and Texture of Materials (Ministry of Education), Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China
paper_title: Fatigue properties of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel: Critical impact of κ-carbide precipitation state
citation_sentences: In the ST sample, the observed planar slip bands are primarily attributed to the short-range ordering (SRO) structure within the austenitic matrix. This observation aligns with the “glide plane softening” effect proposed by Gerold and Karnthaler [40]. The strong C-Mn chemical affinity leads to a non-random solute distribution, forming locally ordered clusters [41–43]. When a leading dislocation cuts through such an SRO region, it disrupts the local order and reduces the resistance on that specific glide plane, thereby facilitating subsequent coplanar glide and the formation of distinct planar slip bands [44–46]. However, because SRO structure is relatively weak and spatially heterogeneous, repeated shearing by moving dislocations tends to destroy it prematurely. The resulting non-uniform softening across potential slip planes fosters local strain concentrations (Fig. 12a).

Aging treatment induces coherent ordered L12-structured intragranular κ-carbides that amplify “glide plane softening” effect beyond the SRO level. When moving dislocations shear these precipitates [47], anti-phase boundaries (APBs) are created. After a plane has been swept and order locally disrupted, subsequent dislocations traverse the same plane with reduced resistance because additional APBs are no longer formed [42]. This path-dependent process channels dislocation activity to a limited set of previously softened glide planes, strengthening slip planarity and suppressing cross-slip. The uniform nanoscale dispersion of κ-carbides promotes the homogeneous formation of planar slip bands and distributes plastic strain over multiple coplanar systems, thereby mitigating localized strain concentrations. Crucially, this enhanced slip planarity and restricted cross-slip significantly improve the reversibility of dislocation motion under cyclic loading [44]. Dislocations can more readily retrace their path on well-defined softened planes during load reversal, leading to reduced accumulated damage per cycle (Fig. 12b). These mechanisms rationalize the superior fatigue performance of the aged samples relative to the ST samples.

citation_sentences: At low stress amplitudes, the fatigue crack initiation stage occupies a significant portion of the total fatigue life; strengthening the matrix is therefore an effective route to delay initiation [48,49]. In Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys, precipitation strengthening by intragranular κ-carbides is dominant, and the active dislocation-precipitate interaction depends on the size of precipitates. The dominant mechanism transitions from shearing to Orowan bypassing as the precipitate size increases, and a critical size of approximately 27 nm was reported in analogous alloy systems [50]. In the present work, intragranular κ-carbides in both the 550-6 and 650- 6 samples (with average sizes of \~11 nm and \~24 nm, respectively) remain in the shearing regime (Fig. 13a,b). Fig. 13(c,d) present highresolution TEM (HRTEM) images of κ-carbides in the fatigued 550-6 and 650-6 samples, respectively. Fig. 13(c',d') are magnified views of the areas marked by green rectangles in Fig. $^ \mathrm { 1 3 ( c , d ) }$ and the corresponding inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) filtered images of the selected regions are shown in Fig. $^ \mathrm { 1 3 ( c " , d " ) }$ , where the κ-carbides, austenitic matrix, and transition regions are delineated in white, black, and blue, respectively. The presence of shear steps and localized contrast variations within the carbides (indicated by red arrows) suggests a disruption of the ordered structure and the formation of APBs, which is consistent with the view that repeated dislocation shearing causes mechanical dissolution of κ-carbides during cyclic loading, as also reported by Wegener et al. [22].

paper_title: Fatigue properties of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel: Critical impact of κ-carbide precipitation state
citation_sentences: The dislocation distribution is clearly demonstrated by TEM characterizations of microstructures adjacent to grain boundaries after fatigue at a $\Delta \sigma / 2$ of 315 MPa (Fig. 14). In the 550-6 sample (Fig. 14a), κ-carbides are uniformly distributed within the grains, with no coarse precipitation at the grain boundaries. These intragranular κ-carbides facilitate organized planar slip bands, promoting relatively uniform slip transfer across grain boundaries [26]. By contrast, the 650-6 sample exhibits coarse intergranular κ-carbide precipitation disrupting the continuity of grain boundaries (Fig. 14b). These brittle particles act as strong slip barriers, intensifying dislocation pile-ups, promoting the formation of tangled dislocation structures with wavy-slip patterns, elevating local stress concentration, and eventually leading to earlier crack nucleation and shortened fatigue life.
